pred_reason: SWC-101 applies due to the reentrancy vulnerability in the Release function where external calls precede state changes. SWC-107 applies because the ResetConfiguration function trusts external contract return values without validation, enabling malicious input manipulation. Evidence: Release function makes external calls before state changes (lines ~104-109), violating checks-effects-interactions pattern.; ResetConfiguration function blindly trusts external contract return values (lines ~84-88) without input validation.; Both findings directly correspond to SWC-101 (reentrancy) and SWC-107 (external call trust) vulnerability patterns.

findings.Description: The Release function makes external calls to untrusted ERC20 contracts before any state changes, violating the checks-effects-interactions pattern. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Attackers could re-enter the contract during token transfers to manipulate state and potentially drain funds.

findings.Description: ResetBot and ResetKeeper functions allow immediate transfer of privileged roles without confirmation, creating centralization risks. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Compromised admin keys could lead to permanent contract takeover and complete loss of funds.
findings.Location: ResetBot and ResetKeeper functions, lines ~106-110 and ~101-105

findings.Description: The totalSupply function multiplies an external token balance by a large constant without overflow protection. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Function will revert when weth[0] balance is sufficiently large, breaking integrations and token economics.

findings.Issue: External Call to Untrusted Configuration Source
findings.Description: ResetConfiguration function blindly trusts return values from an external contract without validation. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Malicious contract could return arbitrary addresses, compromising critical configuration parameters.

findings.Description: EncryptedSwap function allows privileged accounts to arbitrarily manipulate token balances without restrictions. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Can create or destroy tokens arbitrarily, potentially devaluing the token or manipulating balances.

findings.Description: Bot or keeper can assign themselves to each other's roles, undermining intended role separation. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Consolidation of privileged functions under a single entity, creating centralization risks.
findings.Location: ResetBot and ResetKeeper functions protected by BotPower modifier
